new_findings.topic: PPV value ladder and first-sale anchoring
new_findings.title: Value ladder starting at sub-$5 to condition purchasing habit before escalating price

variants.claim: The first PPV sale should always be $5 or less to condition the subscriber to buy; subsequent sales increase in price incrementally, and the overall set is structured as 7 separate sales attempts at progressively higher price points.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are instructed to structure one content 'set' as 7 distinct sales opportunities (individual pics, pic+video combos, etc.) with prices rising at each step. The first sale is always ≤$5 — not because the content is cheap, but to make the buy frictionless and establish a Pavlovian habit: sub sees PPV → sub unlocks. Subs who won't spend $5 are deprioritized or ignored. New chatters are taught to use small price increments (e.g., $5 → $7 → $10) rather than jumping to $25+. Selling too much too soon is explicitly called out as a sub-burning mistake; longevity and lifetime value are the stated goal.

variants.claim: Pricing the first PPV low (roughly $5-$20) exists to get the fan used to spending, not to make money on that sale.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard caps the first PPV at $5-$20, never higher, to get the customer used to the habit of buying on the platform before laddering up; habibi tests a brand-new buyer with a small first buy (e.g. $9) to read his boundaries.

variants.claim: Starting the PPV ladder at a low price ($15) minimizes the psychological barrier to the first purchase, making it far more likely the subscriber enters the escalation funnel and ultimately spends $300–$500.
variants.what_they_do: The specific ladder cited: $15 → $30 → $60 → $120 → $200 → $200. Entry price is low enough to feel trivial. Each step roughly doubles, creating anchoring where each subsequent price feels proportional to the last rather than shocking. The final $200 items are 'full action' — maximum explicitness justifying maximum price. Total session value: ~$625 at full conversion.

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

variants.claim: Selling via an escalating price ladder rather than one high ask raises total spend while keeping each step psychologically easy.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard runs a default ladder of 15, 25, 45, 65, then 100-120, and warns that hitting a warmed-up fan with a single $80 PPV makes him leave for no money, where the same total is extracted reliably in small rising steps.

sources.quote: the first sale should be under $5 should be $5 or less why because everyone can afford $5
seven separate sales so it could be one you know number one could be one pick number two could be a pick in a video
price the very first pbv between $5 to $20 let's say not go any higher than that... get them used to spending
the last one nine bucks this was a first buy for him... I wanted to test his boundaries
you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200 the next one for 200
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber

you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100
We'll start at 15 we'll then go to 25 we'll then go to 45 we'll then go to 65 and... 100 or 120

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented PPV pricing model at Palm (§7 GAP). The '7 sales per set' structure and sub-$5 entry point are concrete, adoptable mechanics. Palm's outsourced chat team may have their own approach, but it is undocumented internally.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt this ladder as Palm's default PPV pricing framework when building the in-house chat team. Codify: (1) first PPV always ≤$5, (2) 7 sales-attempt structure per set, (3) incremental price increases per sale, (4) subs who decline the entry price are de-prioritized. Feed this into the Fan Tracker (§9) so chatters can see where each fan sits on the ladder and what the next logical price point is.

new_findings.title: Inject scripted rapport breaks and push-pull frames throughout chat to create chase dynamic

variants.claim: Deliberately breaking rapport with playful negative statements (e.g., 'I was starting to like you until you said that') and push-pull phrasing prevents the sub from losing interest and causes them to actively seek the chatter's validation — which is the precondition for purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are drilled on two borrowed-from-pickup techniques: (1) Rapport breaks — any statement that actively discourages the deepening of connection, always softened as a joke (e.g., 'you and I would never get along because you're a math teacher and I have such trauma about math teachers'). These are not standalone — they must be followed up or contextualized so they don't read as mean over text. (2) Push-pulls — a positive statement immediately followed by a negative, or vice versa (e.g., 'you're actually kind of funny and I bet people teased you for it growing up'). These are to be peppered throughout the entire conversation, not saved for one moment. The psychological goal: sub chases chatter's validation, which increases desire to please — i.e., spend money.

variants.claim: Letting the fan initiate and withholding rather than over-giving keeps the model in the power position and increases buying.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard plays hot-and-cold and sends non-nude teasers so the customer initiates, putting the chatter in the power position; habibi withholds (tells a fan asking for photos 'no, you're going to have to wait') to drive desire before selling.

sources.quote: a break in rapport anything that actively discourages the deepening or establishment of a positive connection
you should be doing push pulls you should really be doing breaks and rapport or specifically push pulls throughout the entire interaction
we always want to let the customer initiate... we are the ones that are in the power position
even if a guy says hey can you send me photos of your tits you just say simply no you're going to have to wait

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. This is a differentiating technique that separates high-performing chatters from low ones and is absent from most agency training.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include rapport breaks and push-pull scripting as a dedicated module in Palm's in-house chatter training. Provide 10–15 templated examples per technique that chatters memorize, then practice in role-play before going live. Monitor for correct usage in chat reviews — a chatter who never uses these is likely glazing, which suppresses sales.

new_findings.title: Train chatters on a fixed funnel: questions, imposition, pull-push, guilt trips

variants.claim: DM revenue depends on running every fan through a fixed pre-sale framework (data-collection questions, position check, pull-push flirting, then scripted PPV with guilt-trip fallbacks) rather than letting chatters spam PPVs.
variants.what_they_do: Trains new chatters on six new-subscriber questions (name, age, location, work, preferences, kinks) logged to notes, an 'imposition' check that the fan is in a buyable situation, a 'pull-push' flirting loop, then the PPV script, with pre-written guilt-trip messages for each failure point; reports a 75-80% unlock rate.

variants.claim: New chatters must be shown explicitly what 'good' looks like via a call plus a short video and documents that lead with the most common beginner errors before anything else.
variants.what_they_do: Run an onboarding call with the new chatter; share a pre-recorded video and 2–3 concise documents. Documents are structured around the most common mistakes (dry replies that kill conversation, overly aggressive/obvious PPV transitions) and use side-by-side examples — 'wrong version' vs 'right version' — for carrying conversations, captioning PPVs, transitioning, etc.

sources.quote: that's why my chatter conversions are 75 to 80% plus
you don't want to let the fan see the sales knife
pull fan in with a flirty comment... and then push them away with a generic question
We put emphasis on common beginner mistakes
examples are king in your documents. Just show this is an example of a chatter doing it wrong. This is an example of a chatter doing it right

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting with no internal SOP/scripts/funnel (§8 OUTSOURCED); this is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house and AI chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture this questions→imposition→pull-push→script→guilt-trip framework as the backbone SOP for Palm's future in-house chat team and as AI-chat training material.

new_findings.topic: Free teaser content as pre-sell warm-up
new_findings.title: Free teaser → $5–15 qualifier → $30–40+ escalation PPV ladder

variants.claim: Sending free 'outfit' or soft-tease images before the first paid offer raises arousal and reduces friction on the first purchase, making the entire escalation ladder more likely to convert.
variants.what_they_do: At the start of each scripted session, the chatter sends 1–2 free images — typically a suggestive but non-explicit 'outfit' shot — with no price attached, framed as casual sharing ('what do you think about this outfit?'). Only after the subscriber reacts positively does the chatter pivot to the paid ladder. This gates the sell behind a micro-engagement that confirms interest and builds investment.

variants.claim: Starting PPV with a free non-revealing image, then a small $5–15 paid image, then escalating to $30–40+ video creates a progressive upsell funnel that qualifies spenders before committing to expensive content.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter sends (1) a free, non-revealing image to open the conversation; (2) a slightly more revealing image/clip priced $5–15 to qualify whether the fan will spend; (3) subsequent escalating PPV pieces priced $30–40+. Welcome/intro message is auto-sent at $15–25 on subscribe. Each step is treated as a 'drink at the bar' — the fan self-selects into a higher spend tier organically.

sources.quote: I'll maybe send you a couple pictures just for free no price attached of my outfit maybe I look cute I'm like yeah what do you think about this outfit
ideally it's something sexual that makes the subscriber horny and he's like okay yeah that's hot... now after that you can be like yo do you want me to show a bit more
the next video or picture will be a little more revealing you'll sell that for five to ten dollars and so on and so forth the next one from there will be 30 to 40
the first ppv messages should be pictures that are like five to ten dollars you're just trying to qualify if this guy is a spender or a Time waster

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chat SOP exists at Palm (§8). This specific 'free teaser before first pitch' mechanic is a discrete, concrete tactic to encode into the future chatter SOP and AI chatting state machine.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a mandatory 'warm-up phase' in the chat SOP: 1 free teaser image sent before any PPV offer, followed by a reaction-check message. Only proceed to the $15 entry PPV after a positive subscriber response. This applies directly to AI chatting — build the state machine so it cannot enter 'sell mode' before completing the warm-up phase.

new_findings.title: Log personal fan details in notes so any chatter can continue the relationship seamlessly

variants.claim: Chatters add personal details (job, location, interests) to each fan's notes or rename field so any chatter who picks up the conversation later can maintain the illusion of a continuous, invested relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After a fan shares personal information in DMs, the chatter immediately records it in that fan's notes inside the OF messaging interface (or an external CRM). The fan's name field may be renamed to include key tags. When a different chatter takes over a shift, they review notes first and reference those personal details naturally — the fan feels remembered and valued, which increases LTV.

variants.claim: Building plausible commonalities with subscribers — using a tiered system from direct 'me too' to loose tangential connections — is the single most important driver of repeat purchasing.
variants.what_they_do: After qualification, chatters build commonalities using three levels: Level 1 ('me too') — chatter claims to share the same trait directly (e.g., 'I also study psychology'). Used sparingly because it's hard to track across many subs. Level 2 ('I know someone who') — chatter links via a third party ('My little brother studies psychology'). Common but overuse feels weird. Level 3 (tangential connection) — chatter links the sub's trait to something loosely related ('I read a Harvard Business Review article about students who study under 2 hours a night…'). This is the core technique — it's harder to fake, opens new conversational threads, and doesn't require the chatter to claim personal overlap. The goal is to give a 'plausible reason' for emotional connection so that future sales requests don't feel transactional.

sources.quote: we'll have our chatting team add notes to each fan where you can write down personable information and the next chatter that hops on to talk to that dude will kind of have an idea
maybe he's told the girl what he does for a living where he lives and we'll put all that information in his notes or in his name
building commonalities this is going to be the most important section in this presentation
oh really that's interesting I was reading an article in the Harvard Business review and it said that students who study under 2 hours a night do 50% worse

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting tools (§9), but no written retention/chatter handoff SOP. The notes-per-fan mechanic described here is a direct operational input for the Fan Tracker and for the in-house chat team SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Formalise a 'fan card' field set in the Fan Tracker (or OF notes) — at minimum: spending tier, personal details shared, last PPV purchased, last contact date. Require the external chat team to populate this today; make it mandatory in the in-house chat SOP. Aligns with and extends §9 whale-hunting data.

new_findings.topic: Cold outreach via Instagram personal brand
new_findings.title: DM prospects from a personal-looking account, not an agency page

variants.claim: Setting up a credible Instagram profile with professional photos (not casual/topless) is the primary vehicle for cold-outreaching creators, and showing your face builds trust that converts to signed deals.
variants.what_they_do: Agency owner creates a personal IG account with polished location/lifestyle photos (real or Pinterest-sourced). Shows face for credibility; faceless is acceptable if IG photos are professional. DMs creators who appear stuck, plateaued, or post-bad-agency. Offers a free 1-week trial to prove value, then converts to 50/50. Moves promising leads to FaceTime to close.

variants.claim: Run creator outreach from a personal-looking profile rather than a branded agency account so messages get read.
variants.what_they_do: Send from your personal/duplicated account (lifestyle pics, normal-person vibe), never an 'OFM manager' agency account, because models skim their request folder and skip anything that reads as an agency. Stop labeling yourself 'OFM manager' anywhere; call it 'talent/influencer management' instead so you aren't lumped in with the spam.

sources.quote: you want to just make an Instagram profile, you want to set it up some nice business photos
you can offer if you find a model and you believe in yourself… I'll work for free for you for a week and if things improve then we can go to a 50/50 split
most people send their messages out from like an agency account... I would actually highly advise you to do your personal account.
please stop saying OFM manager in either your username, your bio... try to be a little bit different... talent management agency.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led outbound (§6) but no documented IG persona setup, photo standard, or structured funnel from IG profile → DM → FaceTime → sign. The free-trial-then-sign tactic is not in Palm's playbook.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a Palm acquisition IG profile standard: photo style, bio copy, niche positioning. Add a free-trial offer as a low-friction entry for creators already earning but skeptical. Build a short outreach sequence: IG DM → value hook → FaceTime close → contract. Cheap to implement, directly addresses §6 gap.

new_findings.title: Length-based PPV pricing ladder: $5–$8 short, $8–$20 long, nothing under 1 min

variants.claim: Pricing PPV content by video length with a hard floor of 1 minute and a practical ceiling of ~$20 maximizes purchase rate across different fan spending levels.
variants.what_they_do: Short or clothed videos: under $8. Longer or more explicit videos: $8–$20. Hard rule: never charge for content under 1 minute — that goes free. Photos posted free to subscribers (subscription covers them); photos only charged if custom. Sends a mix of price points so lower-budget fans still convert on cheaper items while high-spenders can hit the $20–$25 ceiling.

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: if I send out a shorter video or like a twerking video or I'm clothed then it's somewhere under like eight dollars and if it's a little bit more than that then it's somewhere over eight dollars always under I would say twenty dollars
you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing model (§7 — #1 gap flagged). No internal baseline exists for price ladders, anchoring, or length-based rules. This is pure learn-from-others territory.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a default PPV pricing framework for all Palm creators: <1 min = free/engagement only; 1–3 min = $5–$8; 3–8 min = $8–$15; 8+ min = $15–$25. Document as a pricing SOP and share with the external chat team so they apply consistent pricing in DMs. Review per-creator after 30 days and adjust based on conversion data already in the system.

new_findings.title: Manage creator expectations week-by-week or they invent their own

variants.claim: Explicitly set what will happen in week 1, month 1, and month 2 so the creator doesn't form unrealistic expectations and churn.
variants.what_they_do: On the onboarding call, walk through the timeline for the first week and first two months. Rationale: 'if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own,' which are usually unrealistic and lead to early departure when results don't match.

variants.claim: A documented model profile — covering chatting tone, physical stats, hobbies, and personality details — is essential so chatters can maintain consistent fan relationships without breaking immersion.
variants.what_they_do: For every creator they build a profile that includes: existing chatting style (especially for account transitions), height, age, bra size, feet size, astrological sign, hobbies, and other facts that come up naturally in DM conversations. Chatters reference this doc before and during conversations. Content is stored in a shared Dropbox/Google Drive the model can also upload to directly.

sources.quote: if you don't manage people's expectations, that's when they make their own expectations, which typically is not really reasonable... they leave.
you need to understand their chatting style if especially if they're running their existing of account and you need to transition their chatting style to your Chatters
little things like their height age bra size feet size astrological sign hobbies things that just naturally get brought up during these conversations

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented expectation-setting framework in onboarding (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Bake a week-1/month-1/month-2 expectation script into the onboarding call to reduce early churn.

new_findings.title: Price identical content by traffic source and proven spend

variants.claim: Operators charge different prices for identical content based on the fan's traffic source and proven spend, deliberately under-pricing lower-quality traffic.
variants.what_they_do: X-sourced subs (lower quality, free subs) tagged and sold PPVs at roughly half the price of IG subs; proven spenders and whales quoted higher anchors; price never discussed openly, instead gauged from chat, fan notes and tip-baiting ('show me what you think I'm worth').

sources.quote: you can target them with lower price content to increase your spend per sub
we use differential pricing to target the X subs at lower lower prices

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/commission/PPV model (§7 THIN — 'the #1 creator concern...our position isn't written down').
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a source- and spend-based PPV pricing matrix for real creators now and bake it into chatter guidance; a direct fill for the §7 pricing gap.

new_findings.title: Treat the vault as inventory: weekly script refresh and analytics pruning

variants.claim: Top operators run a weekly operating system that constantly adds new PPV scripts and prunes underperformers by analytics so whales never exhaust the content.
variants.what_they_do: Weekly: add 1-2 new sexting scripts, generate fresh wall teasers, log what content/price performs, replace underperformers (scripts that cause unsubs), keep the vault on-niche and price-test ranges — because a whale burns through 3-4 scripts fast and an empty vault leaves money on the table.

sources.quote: You need to always stay on top of refreshing your scripts
a high quality vault from day one

palm_comparison.vs_us: OF vault/PPV is Palm's thinnest area with no system (§2 THIN); fan/retention analytics is partial (§9). No documented vault-as-inventory refresh loop.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Spec a vault-inventory cadence (weekly script adds + analytics-driven pruning + price testing) for the future AI line and in-house chat/vault build, wired to fan analytics (§9).

new_findings.topic: Emotional experience framing vs. transactional selling
new_findings.title: Sell the intimate experience, not the content — fans pay for emotion, not pornography

variants.claim: Subscribers don't spend money on OF because they can't find free explicit content; they spend because they're buying an emotional, intimate experience that free sites can't replicate.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are trained to frame every interaction as building genuine intimacy — shyness, excitement, escalating personal connection — rather than a content transaction. The chatter script is designed so the fan feels like the experience is happening *with* the creator, not being sold a product. The script's pacing (slow → fast) mirrors real intimacy. This psychological framing is positioned as the core reason scripted chats outperform both mass PPVs and one-off custom content.

sources.quote: only fans isn't about sexuality it's about emotions and experiences
what he really searches for are the emotions the real sexual experience not just the pure sexuality

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal script or framing doctrine. This 'experience over transaction' principle should anchor chatter training philosophy when Palm builds in-house. Also directly applicable to AI chatting — arguably more so, since AI chatters must compensate for lack of a real person.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Make 'sell the experience, not the content' the #1 principle in the chatter training SOP. Require chatters to spend a defined minimum number of exchanges on warmup/small talk before any PPV offer. For the AI chatting project, build emotional escalation cues into the conversation state machine — track where in the 'intimacy arc' each fan is before triggering a sell.

new_findings.topic: Inbound-pull DM strategy vs. mass PPV blasting
new_findings.title: Use feed posts as DM bait to pull fans inbound rather than blasting PPV

variants.claim: Posting a simple 'I'm online / bored' caption on the creator's feed draws fans into DMs on their own initiative, giving the chatter frame control and avoiding the 'spammy' feel of mass PPV messages.
variants.what_they_do: Instead of mass-sending PPV blasts, the chatter (or creator post) publishes a low-key feed post ('hey I'm up, who's up?', 'come to my DMs right now') that signals availability without directly soliciting money. Fans who respond have self-selected as engaged; the chatter is now in a reactive, high-authority position to run the upsell sequence.

sources.quote: post on your page say hey I'm online hey I'm bored um just post a picture caption it and they'll get the idea that you're online
you don't want to ever spam with ppv this is a huge No-No it cheapens your brand

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented DM-pull strategy or mass-message cadence guidance (§8 outsourced, §5 GAP on traffic/distribution strategy). This inbound-pull framing is a complementary tactic to whatever the external chat team is doing and is relevant for the in-house roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Incorporate 'availability bait' posts as a scheduled content type in the Publer/Airtable pipeline — short captions designed to trigger DM inflow, not tied to a content asset. Brief the external chat team on this so they're primed when the flood comes, and document it as a standard practice for the in-house chat SOP.

new_findings.title: Configure an auto-sent paid intro message ($15–25) to fire on every new subscriber

variants.claim: Setting up an automated PPV intro message that triggers the moment a fan subscribes generates passive revenue from every new sub with zero chatter effort.
variants.what_they_do: Inside OF settings, a welcome message is pre-loaded with a PPV attachment priced at $15–25. It fires automatically to every new subscriber. No chatter action required. This is the first monetization touchpoint before any live chat begins, and it also flags whether the new sub is a likely spender (purchase = qualified lead for the upsell ladder).

sources.quote: another way that you can quickly gauge how interested a fan is and your content is if they purchase your intro message this is a message that you set up to automatically send out once they hit subscribe
usually our welcome messages are like 15 to 25 bucks but then the first ppv we send is like 5 to 15

palm_comparison.vs_us: No Palm-documented equivalent (§8 outsourced, §7 GAP on PPV setup). This is a zero-marginal-effort revenue lever that the external chat team can implement immediately for every Palm creator, and should be baked into the in-house chat onboarding checklist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Immediately brief the external chat team to verify every Palm creator has an auto-send intro PPV configured at $15–25. Make this a checklist item in creator onboarding (§6 form wizard). Track intro-PPV purchase rate per creator as a leading indicator of subscriber quality.

new_findings.title: Personalized openers using username or profile detail to force engagement

variants.claim: Sending a message personalized to a specific subscriber's username or visible profile detail dramatically increases response rates versus generic openers.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to craft a unique opener for each sub that contains a play on the subscriber's username (e.g., 'Hey Jimmy, are you the same Jimmy Johnson from the Dallas Cowboys?'). The opener must include exactly one question — no more — so it opens a single conversational thread the sub feels obligated to answer. Copy-paste openers like 'hey how are you' or 'thanks for the follow' are explicitly banned. The opener is designed to (a) prove a human is typing, (b) put the chatter in conversational control by triggering a reaction, and (c) give the sub an easy, interesting thing to respond to.

sources.quote: send a personalized opener that is unique to the person who is receiving it
it must contain one question but no more than one question

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's DM monetization is fully outsourced to an external chat team (§8); there is no internal SOP or opener framework at Palm. This is pure build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat team roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm builds its in-house chat team, mandate personalized username-based openers as the baseline standard. Create a training doc that (1) defines what counts as a valid personalized opener, (2) lists banned copy-paste patterns, and (3) drills the one-question rule. Include this in pre-training screening — candidates who default to 'hey how are you' should be filtered.

new_findings.title: Write immersive, boner-test PPV descriptions that sell the fantasy before the sub sees the content

variants.claim: Long, vivid PPV descriptions that pass a 'can you picture it with your eyes closed' imagination test and a 'does it arouse' boner test dramatically increase unlock rates because the sub is buying stimulation from the description itself, not the preview.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters are required to write out full descriptions for every PPV — not 'here's a pic of my boobs babe' but a multi-sentence, immersive description of camera angle, positioning, what the model is doing, and what the sub should imagine. Two quality tests: (1) Imagination test — can someone visualize exactly what is in the content just from the words, with no preview? (2) Boner test — does the description itself arouse? If a male chatter reads it and finds it hot, it will likely work. Pre-writing descriptions for an entire set before the selling session begins is standard practice. This is treated as a core skill, not an afterthought.

sources.quote: they are not vivid they do not stimulate the subscriber's imagination
does the description vividly describe what is in the content… will the description give the sub a boner when they read it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal PPV copywriting standard (§2 THIN, §7 GAP). This is directly actionable: even before building an in-house chat team, Palm could create a PPV description template and writing guide for the external chat team and for creator-facing content briefing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a PPV description writing guide with the two-test framework (imagination + boner test) and worked examples contrasting bad ('here's a pic babe') vs. good (fully immersive). Deliver this to the external chat team now as a quick win. When building the in-house team, make PPV description writing a standalone scored exercise in the pre-training filter — candidates who can't write a vivid description after coaching should not progress.

new_findings.title: Use narrative 'gambits' (e.g., broken phone, emergency) to collect money outside standard PPV sales

variants.claim: Chatters can collect money from subscribers through creative life-narrative requests (e.g., 'I dropped my phone, can you help?') that are emotionally compelling and do not require the sub to be in a purchasing mindset for explicit content.
variants.what_they_do: Gambits are defined as non-sexual sales techniques where the chatter creates a plausible real-life reason for the model to need money and asks the subscriber to contribute. Examples: broken phone screen, emergency expense. These are framed as personal requests, not content sales, so they bypass the sub's resistance to PPV unlocking. Key rule: gambits must not violate platform ToS (no meetup offers). The presenter treats this as an advanced technique taught in coaching rather than to new chatters, but frames it as unlocking a completely different revenue channel beyond PPV.

sources.quote: gambits are non-sexual sales techniques for example the broken phone screen gambit
you can come up with any reason for them to purchase from you to send you money any reason that you can think of that does not violate the terms

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal baseline at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward. Palm should be aware this tactic exists and is widely used; understanding it is necessary for the AI chatting project to handle these flows appropriately, and for the in-house team SOP to define what gambits are permitted vs. prohibited.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include gambits as a section in the in-house chatter SOP with: (1) a definition and worked examples, (2) a clear ToS compliance checklist (no meetup implication, no off-platform payment requests), (3) a tiered rollout — teach only to experienced chatters, not new hires. For the AI chatting roadmap, flag that gambit handling requires careful scripting to stay compliant and authentic.

new_findings.title: Volume, clarity, and proof drive content performance

variants.claim: Content performance is governed by three levers — volume (negates luck), clarity (avatar's own language), and proof (converts attention to belief).
variants.what_they_do: Treats every post as a lottery ticket so quantity is the path to quality; insists on speaking the avatar's literal words ('tired of chatting all day') over marketing jargon; and backs claims with documented results. 'If you're not embarrassed by how much content you produce, you're not producing enough.'

sources.quote: volume negates luck... you are not in the content business, you're in the volume business
clarity beats cleverness... speak your avatar's language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social engine is data-driven on what to film (§1 MATURE) but cadence/frequency is not codified (§1 GAP) and messaging clarity/proof are not formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a high-volume posting cadence in the SMM hub (§11) and add avatar-language and proof guidelines to the content pipeline, complementing Palm's inspo-driven ideation.

new_findings.title: Run the agency on a defined six-tool stack

variants.claim: A six-figure operation runs on a defined low-cost software stack: fan CRM, video-call tool, deep-link redirect, editor, anti-detect browser, and Telegram for coordination.
variants.what_they_do: Uses Infloww (fan CRM/chatting/scripts, priced as a capped revenue percentage), Google Meet (model calls), Bouncy.ai deep links to hide OF URLs from Meta/Snap, CapCut (editing), Dolphin Anty anti-detect browser + proxies for Reddit, and Telegram with per-topic subgroups to run the whole team.

sources.quote: what we do is we do this thing called a deep link
the most important one... will be Telegram

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm already has a strong spine (Publer, Airtable, Cloudflare, Telegram routing §0/§5) but lacks a fan-CRM equivalent to Infloww and a deep-link/anti-detect layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Map each function to Palm's stack; close the fan-CRM gap (Infloww-style) and evaluate deep-link redirects for link-in-bio safety. Palm already covers Telegram and scheduling.

new_findings.title: Real models beat AI on connection, customs and platform access

variants.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.

sources.quote: they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid

palm_comparison.vs_us: Tempers Palm's AI-persona thesis (§0/§3): Palm's AI personas still funnel real social connection to OF, but this flags the customs/whale and platform-access limits and the Fanvue payout risk for any future AI-creator line.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep Palm's AI personas as a social/scaling lever while concentrating customs/whale monetization on real creators; treat Fanvue-class platforms and payout reliability as explicit risks for any AI-creator expansion.

new_findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

new_findings.title: Proactively coach creators on IG explicit-content ban risk to prevent account loss

variants.claim: Many creators post explicit content on IG out of ignorance, and the agency's value-add is intervening before the ban happens, not after.
variants.what_they_do: Agency flags explicit-content posting as a ban trigger and actively tells creators to stop. Positions this knowledge as a differentiator when pitching creators — 'I can protect your account' is part of the acquisition hook. General IG compliance coaching (what to post, how often, which platforms) is part of the agency's ongoing service.

sources.quote: some will do really explicit content on IG and we all know now that is not a thing you can do — if you do put this content up on IG your account's going to get banned

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is data-driven for ideation but has no documented compliance coaching SOP for creators around IG content rules. Real-creator IG warmup/cadence is also flagged as a gap (§4 THIN for real creators).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'Platform Rules' module to Palm's creator onboarding: a short brief covering IG explicit-content policy, shadowban triggers, and safe posting formats. Deliver this in the first week. Also use IG compliance knowledge as a sales proof point in acquisition outreach ('we've kept X accounts safe').

new_findings.topic: Price integrity / repackaging instead of discounting
new_findings.title: Never fold on PPV price — repackage content instead of discounting

variants.claim: When a fan doesn't purchase at the stated PPV price, cutting the price cheapens the brand; instead, trim or repackage the same video at a lower price point so it appears to be different content.
variants.what_they_do: If a fan ignores or declines a PPV, the chatter does not reduce the listed price. Instead, they clip the same video to a different length, change whether a thumbnail is shown, or rebundle it, then re-pitch it as a 'different video you might like' at a lower price. This preserves the perceived value of the original piece.

sources.quote: if there's a point where they don't purchase don't fold on your pricing what you can do though is you can trim videos make them different lengths repackage it so it doesn't seem like you are folding

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal PPV pricing model or price-integrity policy is documented at Palm (§7 GAP). This is a concrete tactic for the in-house chat and pricing buildout.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'no-discount, repackage only' rule to the future chat team SOP. Build a small asset library of clip variants (different lengths, thumbnail on/off) for each PPV set so chatters have ready-made repackaged versions to offer.

new_findings.topic: Revenue split between subscriptions vs. DMs
new_findings.title: Benchmark: most OF revenue comes from messages, not subscriptions

variants.claim: The vast majority of a high-performing creator's OF income comes from DM/PPV revenue, not subscription fees — illustrated by a creator earning $68K from subs vs. $300K+ from messages.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry uses this ratio as a core KPI framing: subscriptions are the door, DM monetization is the business. They orient their entire agency operation (chatters, scripts, PPV strategy) around maximising message revenue rather than subscriber count.

sources.quote: only made 68 000 over 300 000 literally from the subscriptions the only fan subscriptions and most of the money as you can see came from the messages themselves

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's invoicing system tracks total creator revenue and commissions (§7) but no documented KPI split between subscription vs. message revenue exists. Palm's content engine (§1) is optimised for top-of-funnel subscriber acquisition; the DM monetisation side is outsourced with limited visibility (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a sub-vs-message revenue split metric to Palm's invoicing dashboard and whale-hunting reports (§9). Use this ratio as a health KPI per creator: if message revenue is <50% of total, the chatting strategy is underperforming. Share this benchmark with the external chat team as an accountability target.

new_findings.title: Hierarchical folder labeling system for OF vault content reuse

variants.claim: Organizing vault content by year/quarter/month/week with descriptive labels enables fast retrieval and reselling to new subscribers.
variants.what_they_do: Creator maintains a Dropbox folder tree (Year → Q1-Q4 → Month → Week → Day), names individual files descriptively (e.g. 'pink outfit 1', 'pink outfit 2'), and mirrors this structure inside the OF vault using OF's native label/folder feature. Only edited, reusable content is saved. Legacy content is batched into a single 'pre-2022' folder and backfilled gradually rather than all at once.

sources.quote: I took everything from before 2022 and I put that in one folder and now I created a new folder and it is called of content 2023 and from there I have q1 2 3 and 4

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content system (§2) is THIN on OF vault/PPV organization. The social pipeline (§1) is mature for top-of-funnel assets but there is no documented system for tagging, retrieving, or reselling vault content. This is a direct gap in the OF vault layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a vault taxonomy standard for Palm-managed creators: Year → Month → content-type label (e.g. '2024-03-booty', '2024-03-face'). Add a vault-labeling step to the existing editor workflow so every uploaded asset is tagged before the task is closed. Build a lightweight 'resell menu' Airtable view that surfaces labeled vault assets by creator for chatter/PPV use.

new_findings.title: Pin a pre-made content menu to OF page to drive passive vault resales

variants.claim: Pinning a priced menu of previously sent content to the OF page lets new subscribers self-serve old vault content without creator intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.

sources.quote: I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented equivalent for a passive vault-resell menu (§2 gap). The chatting function is outsourced (§8), so the external chat team likely has no visibility into a structured content menu either. This is a low-effort monetization layer currently missing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize a pinned content menu for each Palm creator. The editor or ops team maintains it in Airtable (listing vault item name, label, price, thumbnail). Brief the external chat team to reference this menu when fans ask about past content. When the in-house chat team is built (~1yr roadmap), make menu-referencing a core chat SOP.

new_findings.title: Send at least one PPV mass message per day — treat it like TV advertising repetition

variants.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
variants.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.

sources.quote: you should be sending out pay-per-view messages at least once a day if you're not you are probably missing out on a lot of money

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented PPV cadence SOP. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project. The daily-minimum rule is a concrete, codifiable KPI.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a PPV send-frequency KPI to the external chat team's performance brief immediately: minimum 1 mass PPV per creator per day. When building the in-house chat SOP (roadmap), hard-code this as a daily task in the chatter checklist. For the AI chatting project, model automated PPV scheduling at 1× daily with A/B price variation.

new_findings.topic: objection and boundary handling in DMs
new_findings.title: Two-warning boundary script: redirect → stern warning → block to protect creator brand

variants.claim: A scripted two-step response to out-of-scope requests (redirect to alternative, then firm final warning before block) maintains creator boundaries without losing a potential buyer.
variants.what_they_do: Step 1: When fan requests something creator doesn't offer, redirect: 'I don't do that but I do [X] — can I get you a custom video?' Step 2: If fan is pushy after first redirect, deliver a firm second message: 'I've told you once, I'll tell you twice — I'll block you if you continue.' Step 3: Block. Never debates or apologizes. Frames the redirect as a sales opportunity, not a refusal.

sources.quote: if someone's asking for something that I don't do I just say hey I don't actually do that but I do blank if you want to get a custom video or a custom picture whatever it is and I kind of offer them an alternative

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) with no internal scripts or objection-handling SOPs. This is concrete build-toward material for the in-house chat SOP. The redirect-to-upsell framing is especially valuable — it turns a boundary scenario into a custom content sale.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add this two-step boundary script to the future in-house chat team's SOP as a default response template. Share with the external chat team now as a briefing document. For the AI chatting project, model this as a conditional response tree: out-of-scope request → redirect + custom upsell offer → if repeated → block flag for human review.

consensus_risers.title: Pre-built escalating sexting scripts are the sales unit

variants.claim: Top sellers sell DM content via pre-produced, internally-consistent escalation scripts that simulate a live session and raise price as explicitness increases.
variants.what_they_do: Each 'sexting script' is a fixed 4-step bundle (SFW starter, first-tease, jugs-only, full-nude bundle) with 2-3 variations, all shot in one consistent setting/lighting; price escalates per step; ElevenLabs voice notes add realism; script content is never reused in mass-PPV or fans realize it is not live.

variants.claim: A pre-built escalation script that guides the fan through progressively more explicit content at increasing price points generates $300–$500 per subscriber per session vs. a one-off $50 custom video.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters use a fixed script structure: (1) open with small talk / lifestyle warmup, (2) send 1–2 free teaser images to raise arousal, (3) transition to selling with a tiered video ladder — e.g., $15 (tease only) → $30 (slightly more) → $60 (light action) → $120 (more explicit) → $200 (full action). Content explicitness scales with price. Subscriber buys each step sequentially; by the final video, the session is complete and they've spent $300–$500. The same script is reused across all subscribers.

variants.claim: A single well-constructed escalation script can be reused with every subscriber, making chat operations scalable without requiring chatters to freestyle or produce new custom content daily.
variants.what_they_do: The script is treated as a static, reusable asset — not a custom conversation. Chatters are trained to deliver it as if it's personal and spontaneous (the 'experience' framing), but operationally it's the same sequence for every fan. This eliminates the content-production bottleneck of true custom content (where a new video must be filmed for each request) and lets chatters handle higher fan volume with consistent outcomes.

variants.claim: A pre-made DM script that sequences plain messages, half-nude images, and full-nude PPV in a deliberate ladder is the core chatting monetization engine.
variants.what_they_do: Agency writes a reusable script sequence: opener message → follow-up message → half-nude image send → bridging message → full explicit PPV offer → continuation. Recommends doing 6–7 hours of chatting personally first to learn the craft before hiring chatters. Treats this as the primary revenue driver (80–90% of OF revenue).

variants.claim: Monetize via a scripted sequence where each stage is more explicit and priced higher than the last, rather than flat pricing.
variants.what_they_do: A 'script' is pre-made content starting safe-for-work and escalating; price rises each stage, e.g. ~$10-15, then ~$35, ~$70, ~$140, ~$200. Between paid stages, send free photos at the level the fan already unlocked to over-deliver and stand out from other creators.

sources.quote: one fully clothed safer work script starter
use 11 Labs to generate super realistic notes
you have the first video for like $15 second video for 30 then for 60 for 120 for 200... you just continuously progress
if he buys an entire script that's about $300 to $500 you make per subscriber and that's how your channel goes to the big big levels
it's easy to replicate you just have one script ready and you can sell it to every single subscriber
ideally the subscriber gets more and more horny he likes the script more and more he buys everything
you want to make one message, then another, then maybe send her a half nude image, then another message, then a full nude, then blah blah blah
80 to 90% is made through chatting — the models, they're busy creating content, so they don't actually have time to do all the chatting
the first one is fairly cheap, like $10, $15, then it goes to about 35, then maybe 70, 140, and then 200.
you can send a bunch of pictures for free... it's going to seem like you're providing so much more value.

palm_comparison.vs_us: OF vault/PPV is Palm's thinnest area (§2 THIN, no system) and chatting is outsourced (§8 OUTSOURCED); no documented PPV bundle or script structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the escalating-bundle plus consistency rules as the core spec for Palm's future AI-chat and in-house vault production; AI voice-note generation overlaps the active AI-chatting project.

consensus_risers.title: Run a structured onboarding call covering 6 key areas before going live

variants.claim: A thorough onboarding call is the single most important trust-building moment and should cover mindset, content expectations, timeline, invoicing, credentials, and Q&A.
variants.what_they_do: They schedule a dedicated onboarding call and systematically cover: (1) model's mindset and expectations, (2) content volume needed upfront and why, (3) realistic 3-month results timeline, (4) invoicing schedule explanation, (5) credential handover for socials and OF, (6) open Q&A floor for the model. They treat the call as a formal first impression, not a casual chat.

variants.claim: A structured onboarding form (Google Forms) that captures age, location, device model, personal traits, and goals is the operational foundation before any work begins.
variants.what_they_do: Agency sends a Google Form at signing. Fields include: age, country, iPhone model (iPhone 13+ required for IG quality standard), personal traits, content style preferences, and creator goals. Login to OF account follows, then profile audit, promotions review, and a written growth plan.

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

sources.quote: schedule that initial onboarding call and run it with the model this is that first impression
you need to establish some sort of a timeline and expectations as to when they should be able to receive the results
get up a Google Forms, fill it out — you want to know when, how old the model is, where the model's from, if the model's got an iPhone, what iPhone she's recording off
we always do iPhone 13 or above, they have to be, because Instagram care a lot about quality
you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding is a form wizard (name, email, commission %, contract, voice memo) per §6 — it is async and document-driven. There is no documented structured live onboarding call. The form captures admin data but does not walk the creator through expectations, timelines, or content strategy.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a mandatory video onboarding call as Step 2 after the contract wizard. Build a call checklist (Notion/Airtable template) covering the 6 areas above. Record it for reference. This directly addresses the creator-trust gap implied by §6's thin onboarding and supports retention (§9).

consensus_risers.title: Brief chatters on model persona: tone, emojis, typing quirks, niche

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

variants.claim: Chatters must mirror the model's real personality and typing style (excessive letters, cute emojis, pet names) so subscribers cannot detect a VA is behind the keyboard.
variants.what_they_do: The persona section of the training PDF specifies: age range of the model → inferred typing style (e.g. 18–24 = lots of cute emojis, elongated words like 'heyyyy'), the model's content niche, any pet names she uses, and other personality markers. Chatter applies all of these verbatim.

variants.claim: Not all chatters come with polished PPV skills; agencies that hand chatters specific scripts and strategies outperform those that leave chatters to their own methods.
variants.what_they_do: Black Cherry operates a chatting team internally but also licenses their PPV script framework to other agency operators. They provide PDF script playbooks, documented PPV funnels, and course-based training covering at least seven distinct PPV strategies. The agency owns the IP; chatters execute to spec.

sources.quote: some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module
take note of the client's Persona so if the girl is aged between say 18 to 24 then using a lot of cute emojis when typing using a lot of excessive letters in some words
you need to know these strategies so that you can train shatters to be this good not all Chatters have mastered the game they know what they know but when you can give them scripts like this
Down Below there's actually going to be a PDF attached where you can see a very precise example of how we run this

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8) with no documented internal SOP or onboarding material. There is no equivalent persona brief or chatter training kit. This is a direct gap to fill as Palm builds toward an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), create a per-creator ZIP/Notion training module template: persona card (age, niche, tone, emojis, pet names), platform walkthrough, PPV pricing ladder, and a baseline script. Standardize this as the chatter onboarding artifact.

consensus_risers.title: Hard floor of 50/50 revenue split — never go lower

variants.claim: Agencies should refuse any deal below a 50% commission split, framing it as the clean, fair baseline rather than a negotiable starting point.
variants.what_they_do: States a non-negotiable 50/50 split as the minimum: if a creator earns $2K/month, the agency takes $1K. No tiered structures, performance bonuses, or sliding scales are mentioned — flat 50% is presented as the standard.

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: don't go below 50/50 revenue split. If your model makes $2,000 a month, that's 1,000 for her and 1,000 for you. Clean, fair, and simple.
you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline
charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale, floor, or tier model (§7 GAP). The commission field exists in the Creators table but the logic behind what percentage to set is unwritten.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's commission policy explicitly: establish a floor (50% is industry-standard per this and other sources), define whether Palm ever takes less (e.g., for a high-revenue creator where 50% would be disproportionate) and codify the rationale. Add this to the creator onboarding wizard as a displayed policy, not just a blank field.

consensus_risers.title: Free-trial subs, monetize later via PPV — not on the sub price

variants.claim: Operators run free or near-free subscriptions to maximize entries, then make essentially all revenue from in-DM PPV and tips rather than the subscription fee.
variants.what_they_do: Run a 7-day free trial (or $3.99 floor sub) with scarcity framing ('limited spots / offers left'); the sub itself is treated as a bonus while the real money comes from getting fans inside and selling price-locked PPV scripts, tips, wall content and a recurring 'girlfriend experience' (e.g. $500/week VIP).

variants.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
variants.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.

sources.quote: I strongly, strongly stress that you run a free trial. You don't charge for subs
the girlfriend experience
nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented pricing/sub model (§7 THIN); chatting/PPV that this depends on is outsourced (§8).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture the low-friction-entry + monetize-in-DM model and a VIP/girlfriend-experience recurring tier as inputs to Palm's pricing model (§7) and in-house/AI chat roadmap (§8).

consensus_risers.title: Use an Intrigue-Curiosity-CTA opener and book the call fast

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

variants.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
variants.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.

sources.quote: I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.
within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented DM script, send-rate discipline, or follow-up cadence for acquisition (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ICC opener template, a manual-typed/personalized/spaced sending SOP to avoid bans, and a 2-week daily follow-up rule; track per-step conversion.

consensus_risers.title: Tie each team role's compensation to the specific business metric that role drives

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

variants.claim: A commission-only base combined with milestone bonuses, daily ranked leaderboards shared with the team, and regular chat reviews sustains chatter performance over time and is the structural reason revenue grew from ~$20k/month to $80k+.
variants.what_they_do: Daily: an assistant compiles each chatter's individual revenue and ranks them on a leaderboard shared with the whole team — publicly feeding ego and competitive instinct. Comp structure: commission only plus bonuses tied to achievable milestones (the presenter spent three days designing this mathematically; result was a ~25% revenue lift). Chat reviews: manager logs into accounts periodically, reviews a sample of conversations, records a screen-capture with verbal feedback, sends to the chatter.
